tasks.name: Create new page: Member roles developer guide
tasks.description: Create a new developer-facing page documenting the member roles system — the three-tier content access model (Public/Secured/SecuredWithRoles), ContentAccessSettings API, HasAccess() extension method, ContentItemRequiredMemberRoleNames, programmatic role management via IContentItemMemberRoleManager and IContentItemMemberRoleRetriever, and integration with ASP.NET Identity (ApplicationRole, ApplicationRoleStore).

add: Front matter (title, identifier, order, persona)
Introduction — three-tier content access model overview
Content access settings — Public(), Secured(), SecuredWithRoles()
Check content access — HasAccess() extension method
Query role requirements — ContentItemRequiredMemberRoleNames
Manage roles programmatically — IContentItemMemberRoleManager, IContentItemMemberRoleRetriever
ASP.NET Identity integration — ApplicationRole, ApplicationRoleStore
Next steps

docFacts.fact: ContentAccessSettings changed from struct to sealed class. Has static factory methods: Public(), Secured(), SecuredWithRoles(IEnumerable<string> roleCodeNames). Now has == and != operators.

IContentQueryDataContainer.ContentItemRequiredMemberRoleNames: IReadOnlyCollection<string> — new property. ContentItemRolesResultProcessor post-processes query results. Lazy loading: role data only fetched when at least one item has ContentItemIsSecured=true.

ContentItemFieldsSourceExtensions.HasAccess(this IContentItemFieldsSource, ClaimsPrincipal) — public extension method for three-tier access check. Returns are Allowed (200), NotAuthenticated (401), Forbidden (403).

invariants.rule: Every documentation article must contain: Title, Introduction, Body, Result, and Next Steps.
Write an introduction at the beginning of each article providing context, real-world scenarios, and explaining value to the user.
Introduction must answer: What is the primary use case? Why does it benefit me? When should I use it?
Provide next steps at the end of articles, linking to related articles or additional resources.
Each guide must have a clear type label: Concept, Tutorial, or How-to (following Diataxis framework).
Every page must have YAML front matter between --- lines with required fields: title, identifier, order, redirect_from, persona, license (for _documentation and _guides collections).
Identifier must be snake_case base + collection suffix (_xp for _documentation, _guides for _guides). Must be unique across the whole repo.
Order field: start at 100 for new sections; insert by midpoints (550 between 500 and 600).
Valid persona values: developer, admin, business, architect, all.
Use backticks for inline code: API members, configuration keys, methods, namespaces, classes, command line tools and arguments.
Developers and admins: can combine 2 related easy steps. Use developer terminology appropriately. Explain business/marketing terms when needed.
Developer guides: break code examples into logical steps/sections. Tutorials need more detailed code; How-tos need less.

User types: user = admin account holder; member = registered visitor without admin access; visitor = anonymous website user; contact = Xperience entity for visitors. Do not confuse contact with visitor or customer.

acceptanceCriteria: Three-tier access model (Public/Secured/SecuredWithRoles) is clearly explained with code examples (INV-structure-002, INV-structure-003)
ContentAccessSettings factory methods documented with usage examples (REC-001, REC-012)
HasAccess() extension method documented with code sample (REC-014)
ContentItemRequiredMemberRoleNames property documented with query example
IContentItemMemberRoleManager and IContentItemMemberRoleRetriever interfaces documented (REC-001)
ApplicationRole/ApplicationRoleStore integration explained for ASP.NET Identity
Correct front matter: unique identifier, order, persona=developer (INV-frontmatter-001, INV-frontmatter-002)
Uses backtick formatting for all type names (INV-formatting-004, REC-015)
Uses established terminology: member, visitor, user, sign in (INV-content-030, REC-013)
Code examples progress from simple to complex (REC-014)
How-to focused — addresses user goals not API surface (REC-008, REC-009)
Next steps section links to related pages (INV-structure-010)

tasks.name: Create new page: Reference — ContentAccessSettings
tasks.description: Create a new API reference page for the ContentAccessSettings sealed class — documenting the struct-to-class migration (breaking change), static factory methods Public()/Secured()/SecuredWithRoles(), equality operators, null handling, and usage examples.

add: Front matter (title, identifier, order, persona)
Introduction — ContentAccessSettings overview and purpose
Breaking change note — struct to sealed class migration
Static factory methods — Public(), Secured(), SecuredWithRoles()
Comparison table — three access modes side by side
Equality operators — ==, !=, Equals() behavior
Null handling — ArgumentNullException on null assignment, use Public() instead
Usage examples with event handlers and parameter classes
Next steps

docFacts.fact: ContentAccessSettings changed from struct to sealed class. New operators: ==(ContentAccessSettings, ContentAccessSettings), !=(ContentAccessSettings, ContentAccessSettings). Equality is now null-safe with operator overloads.
All 8 content engine event data state classes gain null validation (ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull) on AccessSettings property. Previously accepted null silently.
6 website event data state classes gain same null validation. WebPageMetadata and CreateWebPageParameters also validate non-null.
ContentItemMetadata.AccessSettings and CreateContentItemParameters.AccessSettings now validate non-null.
